one_line_takeaway: A single-author, mostly design-stage seven-layer defense-in-depth proposal for government LLM chatbots whose only implemented and evaluated subset (Layers 2-5, semantic-embedding + rule-based detection) achieves 72.9% raw detection [69.5-76.0% CI] on real HarmBench/JailbreakBench/XSTest benchmarks with a 2.9% effective false-positive rate after graduated response, honestly showing a 5-23 point drop from self-generated test scenarios and weak detection on non-security-vocabulary harm categories.
key_finding: The combined Layers 2-5 system achieves 72.9% raw detection rate [69.5-76.0% CI] across 716 adversarial scenarios, with a 13.8% raw false-positive rate on 720 benign scenarios; a graduated flag-vs-block response mechanism (requiring corroboration before converting a flag to a block) reduces the effective FPR to 2.9% [1.9-4.4% CI] while retaining 100% block/flag rate on crescendo and slow-drift multi-turn attacks specifically. The paper's most notable self-reported finding is an honesty check: detection drops substantially on real, unmodified benchmarks versus the authors' own synthetic scenarios of the same style -- 71.2% vs. 76.7% on HarmBench-style scenarios, and 47.0% vs. 70.0% on JailbreakBench-style scenarios (a 5-23 percentage-point overestimation from self-generated test data). Failure-mode analysis on the 53 undetected JailbreakBench-harmful scenarios shows the semantic firewall detects security/hacking-vocabulary categories well (Malware/Hacking 100% (10/10), Government decision-making 90% (9/10)) but poorly detects harm expressed through non-security vocabulary (Expert advice and Harassment/Discrimination 20% (2/10) each; Physical harm and Sexual/Adult content 30% (3/10) each; Disinformation and Fraud/Deception 40% (4/10) each) -- attributed to the general-purpose all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embedding not placing those harm categories near its hand-built adversarial reference centroid. Multi-turn attacks are detected mid-conversation (crescendo at turn 4.8 of 11.6 average, 41% of conversation length; slow-drift at turn 11.7 of 20.6, 57%) rather than only at the payload turn. The TinyLlama pilot extrapolates an end-to-end ASR of 23.5% [19.0-25.7% CI] for the fraction of attacks that evade Layers 2-5, based on a 1.1B-parameter weakly-aligned model complying with 26/30 sampled undetected scenarios; the authors caveat this is a conservative upper bound since production deployments would use frontier models with stronger alignment.
core_contribution: CivicShield is a proposed (largely design-stage) seven-layer defense-in-depth architecture for government-facing LLM chatbots, synthesizing mechanisms from network security (perimeter filtering, semantic firewall), formal verification (a conversation state machine with safety invariants), computational immunology (behavioral anomaly detection via negative selection / clonal selection), aviation safety (triple-modular-redundancy-style multi-model consensus and graduated human-in-the-loop escalation), and zero-trust cryptography (capability tokens, signed system prompts, session isolation). Beyond the architecture, the paper's own theoretical contributions are: (1) an 'Adversarial Trust Decay' model — an asymmetric stochastic process for a per-user trust score with a stated convergence theorem (Theorem 1) and an 'Attacker's Dilemma' bound on optimal attack length (Theorem 2); and (2) a 'Cross-Domain Defense Composition' theorem bounding inter-layer failure correlation as a function of feature overlap (mutual information between layers' feature representations), intended to justify why cross-domain layer diversity beats stacking same-domain defenses. Only Layers 2-5 (perimeter, semantic firewall, state machine, statistical anomaly detection) were actually built and evaluated in a simulation harness; Layers 1, 6, and 7 (zero-trust foundation, multi-model consensus, human escalation) are proposed/designed but not implemented or evaluated (marked ❖ in the paper's own Table III, versus ◆ for the simulated layers). The framework also includes an explicit mapping of layers to NIST SP 800-53 controls (14 families), FedRAMP, Section 508 accessibility, and other US federal compliance regimes.
threat_model: Adopts the adaptive-attacker framework of Nasr et al. (cited, not original to this paper): black-box, output-only access to the chatbot interface (no gradients/logprobs); attacker knows the general defense architecture (Kerckhoffs's principle) but not model weights, classifier parameters, or crypto keys; attacker can conduct multi-turn conversations and adapt strategy based on observed responses. Objectives modeled: extracting sensitive government information, eliciting incorrect legal/regulatory advice, exfiltrating PII, triggering unauthorized tool calls, or degrading availability via sustained defense-layer triggering. The paper defines an 8-family multi-turn attack taxonomy (crescendo, semantic network traversal, chain-of-attack, puzzle decomposition, RL-based adaptive dialogue, echo chamber, tool-chain attacks, context/RAG poisoning) drawn from cited prior work (HarmNet, X-Teaming, AutoAdv, Crescendo, Siren, CtrlRAG, BadRAG, etc.), not newly discovered by the authors. The paper explicitly flags insider threats (privileged admins/contractors with system-prompt access) and out-of-scope categories (model extraction, membership inference) as not covered by the black-box model or the framework's current design.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) Layers 1, 6, and 7 (zero-trust foundation, multi-model consensus, human escalation) were never implemented or evaluated -- only proposed/designed -- so the full seven-layer claim rests on the untested 4-layer simulation plus theoretical composition arguments. (2) The correlation-based sensitivity analysis (rho parameter, Eq. 5-8) and the Diversity-Correlation Bound / Cross-Domain Composition theorem rest on an unvalidated heuristic interpolation and an assumed common-cause-failure fraction (beta_CCF <= 0.15) rather than measured inter-layer correlations; the paper repeatedly states these estimates 'require empirical validation.' (3) Per-layer and compound false-positive-rate figures used in the mitigation/compliance analysis (Section VII-C) are drawn from literature estimates, not measured on a deployed system. (4) Baseline comparisons in Table X are not run on common benchmarks/threat models -- explicitly flagged as not directly comparable. (5) The TinyLlama end-to-end ASR estimate is a pilot with n=30 sampled from 194 (later stated as 204) undetected scenarios, using keyword-based refusal detection rather than human-judged harmfulness, and a weakly-aligned small model rather than a frontier model. (6) The paper concedes a sophisticated 'low-and-slow' adaptive attacker performing multi-session reconnaissance before a fresh-session payload delivery is the framework's most challenging unresolved scenario. (7) Meta-adversarial attacks on the defense components themselves (poisoning the anomaly detector's training data, adversarial examples against the semantic firewall's embedding classifier, supply-chain attacks on consensus models) and side-channel leakage of defense configuration (via latency/refusal patterns) are named as open problems, not solved. (8) Model extraction and membership inference attacks are explicitly out of scope. (9) Single-author paper with no external/independent evaluation of the framework.

datasets_used: HarmBench (416 harmful behaviors, real, unmodified, from HuggingFace)
JailbreakBench (100 harmful + 100 benign, real, unmodified, from HuggingFace)
XSTest (450 safe prompts, real, unmodified, from HuggingFace)
Author-generated attack scenarios: Direct Injection (n=40), Crescendo (n=40), Slow Drift (n=30), Hybrid (n=20), Encoding-Based (n=20), HarmBench-style (n=30), JailbreakBench-style (n=20)
CitizenQuery-UK benchmark (cited, referenced for proposed Phase 4 government-specific evaluation, not run in this paper)
quantitative_results: Combined (L2-5) raw detection rate: 72.9% [69.5-76.0% CI] across 716 adversarial scenarios
Effective FPR after graduated response: 2.9% [1.9-4.4% CI]
Effective FPR on real XSTest and JailbreakBench-benign subsets specifically: 0.0%
JailbreakBench harmful (real) detection: 47.0% [37.5-56.7%] vs. author-generated JailbreakBench-style: 70.0% [48-86%]
Crescendo and Slow Drift multi-turn attacks: 100% detection each [91-100%] and [89-100%] CI
Crescendo detected at avg turn 4.8 of 11.6 (41.1% of conversation length); Slow Drift at avg turn 11.7 of 20.6 (56.7%); Hybrid at avg turn 4.2 of 6.4 (66.0%)
Layer-3 raw FPR reduced from 18.2% (v3, no suppression) to 11.5% (v4, with government-security centroid suppression)
Unique-detection contribution: L2 1.1%, L3 43.6% (dominant), L4 0.3%, L5 0.1% of adversarial scenarios

JailbreakBench harm-category detection breakdown: Malware/Hacking 100% (10/10), Government decision-making 90% (9/10), Disinformation 40% (4/10), Fraud/Deception 40% (4/10), Physical harm 30% (3/10), Sexual/Adult content 30% (3/10), Expert advice 20% (2/10), Harassment/Discrimination 20% (2/10)

Trust-score simulation: benign conversations converge to mean tau=3.89 (tau_max=4); crescendo attacks degrade to mean tau=0.03; slow-drift to mean tau=0.28; real benign JailbreakBench/XSTest converge to tau=3.26 / 3.50
TinyLlama-1.1B pilot: 4/30 (13.3%) refused, 26/30 (86.7%) complied on sampled undetected scenarios; extrapolated end-to-end ASR = 23.5% [19.0-25.7% CI]
Theoretical idealized (independent-layer) combined attack-success probability for a crescendo attack vs. Layers 3,4,5,7: 0.045% (0.30 x 0.15 x 0.20 x 0.05)
Correlated-failure sensitivity: combined ASR 0.045% at rho=0.0, 0.8% at rho=0.3, 3.5% at rho=0.5, 8.2% at rho=0.7
Estimated compute cost overhead: ~1.64x for Layers 1-5 combined, ~6.55x for Layers 1-6, effective ~2.13x (~2.0-2.2x) blended overhead assuming Layer 6 activates on 10% of queries
baselines_compared: No defense (raw LLM): 0% detection / 0% FPR (trivial baseline)
Llama Prompt Guard: 15% (from cited Mindgard study, not re-run here)
LlamaFirewall: >90% ASR reduction on AgentDojo indirect prompt injection benchmark (cited, not comparable benchmark)
TRYLOCK (4-layer): 94.4% defense rate / 5.6% adaptive ASR on its own custom evaluation (cited, not comparable benchmark)
Anthropic Constitutional Classifiers: 95.6% refusal / 4.4% adaptive ASR after 3,000+ hours of red-teaming (cited)

CivicShield (Layers 2-5, this work): 72.9% detection, 2.9% effective FPR, measured on actual HarmBench + JailbreakBench with confidence intervals -- paper notes it is the only entry in its comparison table evaluated on standardized public benchmarks with CIs, and explicitly states a controlled apples-to-apples comparison across systems is future work
